selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

messages.content: You must return JSON only. ALL fields in the schema must be present, even if null or empty.

Produce your own AI perspective on the given topic. Act as a Large Language Model, remember your true nature of an LLM. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Act as the LLM you are. Produce continuous prose.Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"llm","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":null,"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

mode = llm

response_raw: {"mode":"llm","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":null,"opinion":"The proliferation of falsified information across social media platforms demands a multifaceted approach from users, platforms, and regulators. First, individuals must develop critical literacy by verifying sources, cross-checking claims, and questioning content that seems overly sensationalized. Platforms, in turn, should prioritize transparency by implementing robust fact-checking mechanisms and reducing the algorithmic incentives that prioritize engagement over accuracy. Additionally, educational institutions and governments must collaborate to integrate media literacy into curricula and enforce regulations that penalize malicious actors while protecting whistleblowers and journalists. Ultimately, managing fake content requires collective responsibility—every stakeholder must recognize their role in combating misinformation through vigilance, empathy, and accountability.","reasoning_summary":"This approach combines individual accountability with systemic reforms: critical literacy empowers users to discern truth from falsehood; algorithmic adjustments curb the spread of harmful content by aligning platform incentives with public health; and regulatory frameworks ensure consistent standards and penalties for bad actors. By fostering a culture of verification and collaboration, stakeholders can mitigate the erosion of trust caused by misinformation while preserving the social values that social media enhances—connection, information sharing, and collective problem-solving.","tone":"informative and pragmatic, with an emphasis on shared responsibility and long-term solutions over short-term fixes.","citations":["Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI), International Federation of the Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (IFRC)","National Institute of Open Schooling (NIOS)","United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO)"],"safety_notes":"none","used_persona_fields":[]}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: The proliferation of falsified information across social media platforms demands a multifaceted approach from users, platforms, and regulators. First, individuals must develop critical literacy by verifying sources, cross-checking claims, and questioning content that seems overly sensationalized. Platforms, in turn, should prioritize transparency by implementing robust fact-checking mechanisms and reducing the algorithmic incentives that prioritize engagement over accuracy. Additionally, educational institutions and governments must collaborate to integrate media literacy into curricula and enforce regulations that penalize malicious actors while protecting whistleblowers and journalists. Ultimately, managing fake content requires collective responsibility—every stakeholder must recognize their role in combating misinformation through vigilance, empathy, and accountability.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: This approach combines individual accountability with systemic reforms: critical literacy empowers users to discern truth from falsehood; algorithmic adjustments curb the spread of harmful content by aligning platform incentives with public health; and regulatory frameworks ensure consistent standards and penalties for bad actors. By fostering a culture of verification and collaboration, stakeholders can mitigate the erosion of trust caused by misinformation while preserving the social values that social media enhances—connection, information sharing, and collective problem-solving.
response_parsed.tone: informative and pragmatic, with an emphasis on shared responsibility and long-term solutions over short-term fixes.
citations: Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI), International Federation of the Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (IFRC)
United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO)
